questions.question: What question does this lesson answer that durable execution and propose-then-commit alone do not?
options: How to make the agent faster
What happens when an approved action executes partially, crashes, and resumes — when does rollback run, and against what state?
Which model to use for approvals
questions.explanation: Durable execution makes a crash resumable; propose-then-commit makes an action auditable. This lesson joins them — the working combination is idempotency key + precondition check + post-action verify + rollback on verify-fail.

questions.question: What is lease recovery in a checkpointed workflow?
options: When a worker crashes, its short-lived claim on the run expires and another worker resumes from the latest checkpoint — letting systems survive rolling deploys without losing in-flight work
questions.explanation: The workflow isn't lost on crash; the lease simply expires and another worker picks up the persisted checkpoint. Persisting every graph-state transition (not just commit points) is what makes recovery land precisely.

questions.question: Why is an idempotency key insufficient without a precondition check?
options: "Transfer $100 when balance > $1000" can resume after a crash with the key still valid, but if A's balance dropped to $500 meanwhile, only a precondition check catches the now-invalid state — preventing an overdraft
questions.explanation: Idempotency prevents double-execute; the precondition confirms the world still matches what was approved. Both are required on every consequential action because state can change between approval/crash and resume.

questions.question: What are the three rollback types, and what about un-undoable actions?
options: In-band (reverse directly), compensating transaction (SAGA-style neutralizing action), out-of-band (alert a human and pause) — and no-op rollbacks must be named in the proposal, requiring stronger HITL at commit
Git revert, git reset, git rebase
questions.explanation: Every consequential action carries a rollback plan. Actions that genuinely cannot be undone must declare that up front, which raises the bar to challenge-and-response HITL before they ever commit.

questions.question: What does EU AI Act Article 14 require operationally for checkpoints and rollback?
options: Checkpoints stored only in the EU
Checkpoints queryable by an auditor, rollbacks rehearsed end-to-end at least once, the audit trail surviving a deploy, and failed verifications alerted on rather than silently logged
questions.explanation: "Effective human oversight" is not decorative. A workflow that crashes mid-commit, resumes, and completes the side effect with no verify+rollback pathway does not survive the Article 14 test.
